8.1.1.intuition: Think of a restaurant: the client is a customer placing an order, the waiter (HTTP) carries it to the kitchen (server), which coordinates cooks (services), pantry (database), and prep station (cache) to assemble the meal (response) and send it back.

exercises.title: Trace a Request
exercises.description: Build an Express middleware that logs every phase of the request lifecycle: when the request arrives, after auth, after DB query, and when the response is sent. Include timestamps to measure each phase's duration.
exercises.hint: Use `res.on('finish', ...)` and store `Date.now()` at each middleware checkpoint using `req.timings = {}`.

exercises.title: Connection Reuse Benchmark
exercises.description: Write a script that makes 100 sequential HTTP requests to a local Express server, first without keep-alive, then with keep-alive enabled. Measure and compare total time.
exercises.hint: Use `node:http` Agent with `keepAlive: true` vs the default agent. Use `console.time` to measure.

exercises.title: Event Loop Blocking Detector
exercises.description: Create a middleware that detects when the event loop is blocked for more than 100ms. Log a warning with the route that caused the block.
exercises.hint: Use `monitorEventLoopDelay` from `node:perf_hooks` or periodically check `Date.now()` drift with `setInterval`.

problems.problem: Requests to the server are timing out intermittently
problems.cause: A synchronous operation (large JSON parse, crypto, or regex) is blocking the event loop, preventing other requests from being processed.
problems.solution: Profile with `clinic doctor` or `--prof` flag. Offload CPU-intensive work to worker threads. Use streaming JSON parsers for large payloads.
problems.symptoms: Some requests complete in <50ms while others take 5+ seconds. Server CPU shows 100% on one core.

problems.problem: High latency on first request after idle period
problems.cause: TCP connections and DNS cache have expired. The first request must re-establish connections (DNS lookup + TCP handshake + TLS handshake).
problems.solution: Enable HTTP keep-alive with appropriate timeouts. Use DNS caching libraries. Implement health check endpoints that keep connections warm.

problems.problem: Express app crashes with 'socket hang up' errors
problems.cause: Upstream service or database closed the connection before the response was sent, often due to timeout mismatch.
problems.solution: Set appropriate timeouts on all HTTP clients and database connections. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff. Ensure server timeout > upstream timeout.

further_reading.title: Node.js Event Loop Documentation
further_reading.note: Official guide to understanding the event loop phases

further_reading.title: High Performance Browser Networking
further_reading.note: Free online book covering TCP, TLS, HTTP/2 and networking fundamentals

further_reading.title: How DNS Works
further_reading.note: Visual comic explaining DNS resolution step by step

8.1.2.intuition: A load balancer is like a restaurant host who seats customers across available tables. Round robin fills tables in order; least connections seats at the emptiest table; IP hash always gives regulars their favorite table.

exercises.title: Build a Round Robin Proxy
exercises.description: Create a Node.js HTTP server that acts as a round-robin load balancer, forwarding requests to 3 backend Express servers running on different ports. Include health checks that remove unhealthy servers from the rotation.
exercises.hint: Use `node:http` to create the proxy. Use `http.request()` to forward to backends. Periodically ping `/health` on each backend.

exercises.title: Implement Consistent Hashing
exercises.description: Build a consistent hash ring that maps user IDs to servers. Add and remove servers and verify that only a small percentage of keys are remapped.
exercises.hint: Use virtual nodes (100+ per server) for even distribution. Compare key-to-server mapping before and after adding a server.

exercises.title: Nginx Load Balancer Setup
exercises.description: Configure Nginx to load balance across 3 Node.js instances using least_conn algorithm. Add health checks, connection keepalive, and test failover by stopping one instance.
exercises.hint: Use Docker Compose to run Nginx + 3 Node.js containers. Set `max_fails=2 fail_timeout=10s` for passive health checks.

exercises.title: Compare Algorithm Performance
exercises.description: Simulate 10,000 requests across 5 servers with varying response times. Compare round robin, weighted round robin, and least connections. Measure average latency and server utilization.
exercises.hint: Create mock servers with `setTimeout` for variable response times. Track per-server request counts and total completion time.

problems.problem: Uneven load distribution across servers
problems.cause: Using round robin when servers have different capacities, or some requests are significantly more expensive than others.
problems.solution: Switch to weighted round robin (assign weights by server capacity) or least connections (automatically adapts to varying request costs).

problems.problem: Users losing sessions after load balancer deployment
problems.cause: Sessions stored in server memory are not accessible when the load balancer routes the next request to a different server.
problems.solution: Use a shared session store (Redis) or switch to stateless authentication (JWT). Avoid sticky sessions as a long-term solution.

problems.problem: Load balancer health checks passing but requests failing
problems.cause: Health check endpoint returns 200 but the application has a deeper issue (e.g., database connection pool exhausted, disk full).
problems.solution: Implement deep health checks that verify database connectivity, Redis availability, and disk space. Return 503 if any dependency is unhealthy.

further_reading.title: Consistent Hashing Explained
further_reading.note: Visual explanation of consistent hashing with virtual nodes

further_reading.title: AWS Elastic Load Balancing
further_reading.note: ALB vs NLB comparison and use cases

further_reading.title: The Art of Load Balancing
further_reading.note: Interactive visual guide to load balancing algorithms

8.1.3.body:
# Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling

## Introduction

When your application cannot handle the current load, you have two fundamental options: make your existing server bigger (vertical scaling) or add more servers (horizontal scaling). This decision shapes your entire system architecture.

## Vertical Scaling (Scaling Up)

Vertical scaling means adding more resources to a single machine: more CPU cores, more RAM, faster disks.

```
Before:                    After:
┌──────────┐              ┌──────────────────┐
│ 2 CPU    │              │ 16 CPU           │
│ 4GB RAM  │     ──>      │ 64GB RAM         │
│ 100GB SSD│              │ 1TB NVMe SSD     │
└──────────┘              └──────────────────┘
  $40/month                 $640/month
```

**Advantages**:
- No code changes required
- No distributed system complexity
- Simpler deployment and operations
- Data consistency is trivial (single machine)

**Disadvantages**:
- Hardware limits (you cannot buy a 1000-core machine)
- Cost grows non-linearly (2x resources often costs 4x)
- Single point of failure
- Downtime during upgrades

For Node.js, vertical scaling is limited because a single Node.js process only uses one CPU core. You need the cluster module or PM2 to use multiple cores:

```javascript
import cluster from 'node:cluster';
import os from 'node:os';

if (cluster.isPrimary) {
  const numCPUs = os.cpus().length;
  console.log(`Primary ${process.pid}: spawning ${numCPUs} workers`);

  for (let i = 0; i < numCPUs; i++) {
    cluster.fork();
  }

  cluster.on('exit', (worker, code, signal) => {
    console.log(`Worker ${worker.process.pid} died. Restarting...`);
    cluster.fork();
  });
} else {
  // Each worker runs the Express app
  const app = express();
  app.get('/', (req, res) => {
    res.json({ pid: process.pid, uptime: process.uptime() });
  });
  app.listen(3000);
}
```

## Horizontal Scaling (Scaling Out)

Horizontal scaling means adding more machines (instances) to distribute the load.

```
Before:                    After:
┌──────────┐              ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
│ Server 1 │              │ Server 1 │  │ Server 2 │  │ Server 3 │
│ 4 CPU    │     ──>      │ 4 CPU    │  │ 4 CPU    │  │ 4 CPU    │
│ 8GB RAM  │              │ 8GB RAM  │  │ 8GB RAM  │  │ 8GB RAM  │
└──────────┘              └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘
  $80/month                 $240/month (linear cost scaling)
```

**Advantages**:
- Virtually unlimited scaling
- Linear cost growth
- No single point of failure
- Can scale down during low traffic (cost savings)

**Disadvantages**:
- Application must be stateless (or use shared state)
- Distributed system complexity
- Need load balancer, service discovery
- Data consistency challenges

## Making Node.js Apps Horizontally Scalable

The key requirement for horizontal scaling is **statelessness**. Your server must not store any request-dependent data in memory.

```javascript
// BAD: In-memory state prevents horizontal scaling
const sessions = new Map();
const rateLimit = new Map();
const cache = new Map();

app.post('/login', (req, res) => {
  sessions.set(req.body.userId, { loggedIn: true });
  // This session only exists on THIS server instance
});

// GOOD: External state stores
import Redis from 'ioredis';
const redis = new Redis(process.env.REDIS_URL);

app.post('/login', async (req, res) => {
  const sessionId = generateSessionId();
  await redis.setex(`session:${sessionId}`, 86400, JSON.stringify({
    userId: req.body.userId,
    loggedIn: true,
  }));
  res.cookie('sessionId', sessionId, { httpOnly: true });
});
```

### Stateless Checklist

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ State Type          │ Move From Memory To              │
├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
│ Sessions            │ Redis / JWT tokens               │
│ Rate limiting       │ Redis (shared counters)          │
│ Cache               │ Redis / Memcached                │
│ File uploads        │ S3 / Cloud Storage               │
│ WebSocket state     │ Redis Pub/Sub for cross-instance │
│ Scheduled jobs      │ BullMQ (Redis-backed)            │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Scaling the Database

Application servers are easy to scale horizontally. Databases are harder:

```
           Application Layer (easy to scale)
         ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐
         │App 1 │  │App 2 │  │App 3 │  │App 4 │
         └──┬───┘  └──┬───┘  └──┬───┘  └──┬───┘
            │         │         │         │
            ▼         ▼         ▼         ▼
         ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
         │        Database (hard to scale)     │
         │  ┌────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    │
         │  │ Primary │───>│ Read Replica │    │
         │  │ (write) │   │  (read only) │    │
         │  └────────┘    └──────────────┘    │
         └────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Database scaling strategies include:
- **Read replicas**: Route reads to replicas, writes to primary
- **Sharding**: Split data across multiple databases
- **Caching layer**: Redis reduces database load
- **Connection pooling**: PgBouncer manages connections efficiently

```javascript
// Read replica routing with Prisma
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

const writePrisma = new PrismaClient({
  datasources: { db: { url: process.env.DATABASE_PRIMARY_URL } },
});

const readPrisma = new PrismaClient({
  datasources: { db: { url: process.env.DATABASE_REPLICA_URL } },
});

// Route reads to replica, writes to primary
async function getUsers(page, limit) {
  return readPrisma.user.findMany({ skip: (page - 1) * limit, take: limit });
}

async function createUser(data) {
  return writePrisma.user.create({ data });
}
```

## Auto-Scaling

Cloud platforms provide auto-scaling based on metrics:

```
                    Auto-Scaling Group
     ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
     │                                          │
     │  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐           │
     │  │App 1 │  │App 2 │  │App 3 │  + more   │
     │  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘  as needed │
     │                                          │
     └──────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
     Scaling Rules:   │
     CPU > 70% ──────> Add instance
     CPU < 30% ──────> Remove instance
     Request count > 1000/s ──> Add instance
```

For containerized Node.js apps with Kubernetes:

```yaml
# kubernetes HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: node-api
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: node-api
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70
```

## When to Use Which

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Scenario                        │ Strategy                  │
├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
│ Early stage startup             │ Vertical first            │
│ Predictable, moderate growth    │ Vertical + read replicas  │
│ Unpredictable spikes            │ Horizontal + auto-scaling │
│ Global user base                │ Horizontal + multi-region │
│ CPU-bound workload              │ Horizontal (more cores)   │
│ Memory-bound workload           │ Vertical (more RAM)       │
│ Database bottleneck             │ Caching + read replicas   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Real-World Scaling Path

Most successful applications follow this path:

```
Stage 1: Single Server
  App + DB on one machine
  ↓
Stage 2: Separate DB
  App server + Managed DB (RDS)
  ↓
Stage 3: Add Caching
  App + Redis + DB
  ↓
Stage 4: Horizontal App Scaling
  Load Balancer + 2-3 App servers + Redis + DB
  ↓
Stage 5: Database Scaling
  App cluster + Redis Cluster + DB Primary + Read Replicas
  ↓
Stage 6: Microservices + Sharding
  Service mesh + Message queues + Sharded DB
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Premature horizontal scaling**: Adding complexity before you need it. Start vertical, move horizontal when you hit limits.
2. **Forgetting shared state**: In-memory caches, sessions, or rate limiters break when you add a second server.
3. **Not testing with multiple instances**: Always test locally with 2+ instances behind a load balancer before deploying.
4. **Ignoring database as bottleneck**: Scaling app servers while the database is the real bottleneck wastes money.

## Summary

Vertical scaling is simpler but has hard limits and creates single points of failure. Horizontal scaling is more complex but offers virtually unlimited capacity and better reliability. Most production systems use both: vertical scaling for databases and horizontal scaling for stateless application servers. The key to horizontal scaling in Node.js is making your application stateless by moving all shared state to external stores like Redis.
8.1.3.intuition: Vertical scaling is like upgrading to a bigger moving truck when you have too much stuff. Horizontal scaling is like hiring more trucks. One mega-truck has a size limit and if it breaks down you are stuck, but a fleet of trucks can grow without limit and survive individual breakdowns.

exercises.title: Statelessness Audit
exercises.description: Take an existing Express application and identify all in-memory state (sessions, caches, counters). Refactor each to use Redis via ioredis, making the app horizontally scalable.
exercises.hint: Search for `new Map()`, `new Set()`, module-level variables, and `global.*`. Replace with Redis GET/SET operations.

exercises.title: Multi-Instance Testing
exercises.description: Run 3 instances of a Node.js app on different ports with PM2 or the cluster module. Put Nginx in front. Verify that sessions work correctly across instances using a shared Redis store.
exercises.hint: Use `pm2 start app.js -i 3` or configure Nginx upstream with 3 ports. Use `connect-redis` for session storage.

exercises.title: Auto-Scaling Simulation
exercises.description: Build a simple auto-scaler that monitors CPU usage of child processes and spawns/kills workers based on thresholds. Use the cluster module to manage workers.
exercises.hint: Use `os.loadavg()` or `process.cpuUsage()` to monitor load. Fork new workers when load > 70%, kill extras when load < 30%.

problems.problem: Application works with one instance but breaks with multiple
problems.cause: In-memory state (sessions, caches, rate limiters) is not shared between instances. Each instance has its own isolated memory.
problems.solution: Move all shared state to Redis. Use JWT for stateless authentication. Use BullMQ for distributed job processing.

problems.problem: Adding more app servers does not improve performance
problems.cause: The bottleneck is the database, not the application servers. All new servers are still waiting on the same overloaded database.
problems.solution: Add a caching layer (Redis) to reduce DB queries. Add read replicas for read-heavy workloads. Optimize slow queries with EXPLAIN ANALYZE.

problems.problem: WebSocket connections dropping when scaling horizontally
problems.cause: WebSocket connections are stateful and tied to a specific server. New connections may route to a different server than where the room/channel state exists.
problems.solution: Use Redis Pub/Sub as a message broker between instances. Libraries like `@socket.io/redis-adapter` handle this automatically.

further_reading.title: The Twelve-Factor App
further_reading.note: Principles for building scalable, maintainable web applications

8.1.4.intuition: Think of a CDN like a chain of local libraries. Instead of everyone driving to the main library downtown (origin server), each neighborhood has a branch (edge node) with copies of popular books (cached content). DNS is the phone book that tells you which branch is closest.

exercises.title: Cache Header Strategy
exercises.description: Design and implement a cache header strategy for an Express API with: static assets, public product listings, private user profiles, and real-time notifications. Set appropriate Cache-Control headers for each.
exercises.hint: Use `s-maxage` for CDN caching, `max-age` for browser caching, `private` for user data, and `no-store` for real-time data.

exercises.title: DNS Resolution Profiler
exercises.description: Build a Node.js script that resolves a domain name and measures the time for DNS lookup, TCP connection, TLS handshake, and first byte. Compare with and without DNS caching.
exercises.hint: Use the `node:perf_hooks` module and `http.request` with socket events: `lookup`, `connect`, `secureConnect`, `response`.

exercises.title: Edge Function Prototype
exercises.description: Create a simple HTTP proxy in Node.js that simulates edge behavior: check a local cache first, serve stale content with background refresh, and forward cache misses to an origin server.
exercises.hint: Use a Map as the local cache with TTL tracking. On cache hit with expired TTL, return stale data and `fetch` fresh data in background.

problems.problem: Users in different regions seeing each other's cached data
problems.cause: CDN is caching responses without considering the `Vary` header or authentication state. A cached response for User A is served to User B.
problems.solution: Set `Cache-Control: private, no-store` for authenticated endpoints. Use `Vary: Authorization` if caching per-user at CDN (not recommended). Only cache truly public, non-personalized responses.

problems.problem: Stale content persists after deployment
problems.cause: CDN caches have long TTLs and old content is served until the cache expires.
problems.solution: Use content-hashed filenames for static assets (Webpack/Vite do this automatically). For API responses, use short TTLs with `stale-while-revalidate`. Use CDN purge APIs for immediate invalidation.

problems.problem: DNS changes not propagating
problems.cause: High TTL on DNS records means resolvers worldwide have cached the old IP address.
problems.solution: Lower DNS TTL to 60-300 seconds before making changes. Wait for the old TTL to expire before making the DNS change. After propagation, restore the TTL to a higher value (3600+).

further_reading.title: HTTP Caching - MDN
further_reading.note: Comprehensive guide to Cache-Control headers

8.2.1.intuition: Cache-aside is like checking your notebook before asking the teacher (database) a question, then writing down the answer. Write-through is having a secretary who always updates both your notebook and the official record simultaneously. Write-behind is jotting notes on a whiteboard and having someone transcribe them to the official record later.

exercises.title: Implement Cache-Aside with ioredis
exercises.description: Build a generic cache-aside wrapper function that works with any Prisma query. It should support configurable TTL, cache key generation, and automatic JSON serialization.
exercises.hint: Create a higher-order function: `cacheAside(keyFn, ttl, queryFn)` where `keyFn` generates the cache key from arguments.

exercises.title: Write-Behind with BullMQ
exercises.description: Implement a write-behind cache for a view counter system. Increment view counts in Redis immediately and batch-flush to PostgreSQL every 30 seconds using BullMQ repeatable jobs.
exercises.hint: Use Redis HINCRBY for atomic increments. Use BullMQ's `repeat` option to schedule periodic flush jobs.

exercises.title: Cache Stampede Prevention
exercises.description: Implement a cache-aside function with mutex locking to prevent cache stampedes. When multiple requests hit a cache miss simultaneously, only one should query the database.
exercises.hint: Use Redis SET with NX (not exists) and PX (expiry) as a distributed lock. Other requests wait and retry.

exercises.title: Benchmark Cache Patterns
exercises.description: Create a benchmark that compares cache-aside, write-through, and write-behind patterns under different read/write ratios (90/10, 50/50, 10/90). Measure throughput and latency.
exercises.hint: Use `autocannon` or a custom script with `Promise.all` to simulate concurrent read and write operations.

problems.problem: Cache and database have different data (stale cache)
problems.cause: Write operation updated the database but failed to invalidate the cache, or another process updated the DB directly.
problems.solution: Always invalidate/update cache after DB writes in the same try/catch block. Use pub/sub to notify other instances of cache invalidations. Set reasonable TTLs as a safety net.

problems.problem: Database overwhelmed after Redis restart
problems.cause: All cached data is lost when Redis restarts. Every request becomes a cache miss, sending all traffic to the database simultaneously (thundering herd).
problems.solution: Enable Redis persistence (RDB + AOF). Implement cache warming on startup. Use circuit breaker pattern to limit DB queries during recovery. Stagger TTLs to prevent synchronized expiration.

problems.problem: Memory usage in Redis growing unbounded
problems.cause: Cached entries are created but never expire. No eviction policy is configured, or keys are created with patterns that bypass TTL.
problems.solution: Always set TTL on cache entries. Configure Redis maxmemory and eviction policy (allkeys-lru recommended). Monitor key count and memory usage.

further_reading.title: Caching Strategies - AWS
further_reading.note: AWS whitepaper on caching patterns with Redis

further_reading.title: Cache Stampede Prevention
further_reading.note: Instagram engineering on preventing cache stampedes

8.2.2.intuition: Cache invalidation is like keeping a phone book up to date. TTL is reprinting it every year (simple but outdated between prints). Event-based is updating individual entries whenever someone moves (accurate but labor-intensive). The best approach is to update critical entries immediately and reprint periodically as a safety net.

exercises.title: Implement Stale-While-Revalidate
exercises.description: Build a caching function that returns stale data immediately while refreshing in the background. Track metrics on how often stale vs fresh data is served.
exercises.hint: Store data with a separate 'refreshAt' timestamp. If current time > refreshAt, serve data and trigger background refresh without await.

exercises.title: Tag-Based Cache System
exercises.description: Implement a tag-based caching system where each cached item can have multiple tags. Provide methods to invalidate all items matching a tag.
exercises.hint: Use Redis SETs to store key-to-tag mappings. SMEMBERS to get all keys for a tag, then DEL them all.

exercises.title: Multi-Instance Cache Invalidation
exercises.description: Set up two Express instances with local in-memory caches backed by Redis. When one instance updates data, both instances' local caches should be invalidated via Redis Pub/Sub.
exercises.hint: Each instance subscribes to an invalidation channel. On write, publish the invalidated key. On message, delete from local Map.

problems.problem: Cache stampede after invalidation
problems.cause: A popular key is invalidated and hundreds of concurrent requests all hit the database simultaneously to rebuild the cache.
problems.solution: Implement a distributed lock so only one request rebuilds the cache. Others wait and read the fresh cache. Use stale-while-revalidate to serve old data during refresh.

problems.problem: Redis KEYS command causing latency spikes
problems.cause: Using `redis.keys('pattern*')` in production blocks the single-threaded Redis server while it scans all keys.
problems.solution: Replace KEYS with SCAN, which iterates incrementally without blocking. Or use tag-based invalidation to avoid pattern matching entirely.

further_reading.title: Cache Invalidation Strategies
further_reading.note: ByteByteGo overview of cache invalidation patterns

further_reading.title: Facebook Cache Invalidation at Scale
further_reading.note: How Facebook handles cache invalidation across data centers

8.2.3.intuition: Distributed caching is like having multiple refrigerators in different rooms of a restaurant. Redis Sentinel is having a backup fridge that automatically takes over when the main one breaks. Redis Cluster is having specialized fridges — one for meats, one for vegetables, one for dairy — so each holds less but together they store everything.

exercises.title: Redis Cluster Setup
exercises.description: Set up a 3-master, 3-replica Redis Cluster using Docker Compose. Connect with ioredis Cluster client and verify data is distributed across nodes.
exercises.hint: Use the official Redis image with `--cluster-enabled yes`. Use `redis-cli --cluster create` to initialize. Check slot distribution with `CLUSTER SLOTS`.

exercises.title: Multi-Tier Cache
exercises.description: Implement a two-tier cache (in-memory Map + Redis) with automatic promotion and invalidation. Measure hit rates at each tier.
exercises.hint: Track hit/miss counters for L1 and L2. Use LRU eviction for the in-memory tier. Test with a zipfian distribution of key access.

exercises.title: Pipeline vs Sequential Benchmark
exercises.description: Write a benchmark comparing sequential Redis GET operations vs pipelined operations for 1000 keys. Measure latency and throughput difference.
exercises.hint: Use `console.time` to measure. Pipeline should be 10-100x faster due to reducing network round trips.

problems.problem: CROSSSLOT error in Redis Cluster
problems.cause: Attempting multi-key operations (MGET, transactions) on keys that map to different hash slots.
problems.solution: Use hash tags to ensure related keys map to the same slot. `{user:123}.profile` and `{user:123}.settings` will be on the same slot.

problems.problem: Redis Cluster failover causes brief data loss
problems.cause: Asynchronous replication means a replica may not have received the latest writes when promoted to master.
problems.solution: Use WAIT command for critical writes to ensure replication. Accept that Redis Cluster provides 'best effort' durability, not strong consistency.
problems.code: await redis.set('critical:key', value);\nawait redis.wait(1, 1000); // Wait for 1 replica, 1s timeout

8.3.1.intuition: BullMQ is like a restaurant's order ticket system. Waiters (API endpoints) write order tickets (jobs) and hang them on the rail (Redis queue). Cooks (workers) pull tickets and prepare dishes independently. If a dish fails, the ticket goes back on the rail for another attempt.

exercises.title: Email Queue System
exercises.description: Build an Express API that accepts email requests and processes them through BullMQ. Include welcome emails, password resets, and order confirmations with different priorities and retry strategies.
exercises.hint: Create separate job names for each email type. Use priority: 1 for password resets, priority: 5 for welcome emails. Set 3 retries with exponential backoff.

exercises.title: Job Progress Tracking
exercises.description: Create a PDF generation queue where jobs report progress. Build an API endpoint that returns job status and a simple polling client that shows progress updates.
exercises.hint: Use `job.updateProgress(percent)` in the worker. Create GET `/api/jobs/:id` that returns state and progress. Client polls every 2 seconds.

exercises.title: Flow Producer Pipeline
exercises.description: Implement an order processing pipeline using FlowProducer with parent-child jobs: validate payment, check inventory, generate invoice. Parent job runs only after all children complete.
exercises.hint: Use FlowProducer.add() with children array. In parent worker, call job.getChildrenValues() to get results.

exercises.title: Rate-Limited API Calls
exercises.description: Build a worker that calls an external API with rate limiting (max 10 requests per second). Use BullMQ's built-in limiter to respect the API rate limit.
exercises.hint: Set worker limiter: { max: 10, duration: 1000 }. This ensures no more than 10 jobs process per second.

problems.problem: Jobs stuck in 'active' state and never complete
problems.cause: Worker process crashed while processing a job. The job remains in active state because no worker acknowledged completion or failure.
problems.solution: BullMQ automatically moves stalled jobs back to waiting. Ensure `stalledInterval` is set (default 30s). Check worker error handlers and ensure async functions don't silently swallow errors.
problems.symptoms: Dashboard shows growing number of active jobs. No completed or failed events firing.

problems.problem: Redis memory growing unbounded
problems.cause: Completed and failed jobs are kept in Redis forever. With high job throughput, this consumes all available memory.
problems.solution: Set removeOnComplete and removeOnFail options when adding jobs. Use age-based AND count-based limits.

problems.problem: Worker not processing jobs despite jobs being in queue
problems.cause: ioredis connection configured with maxRetriesPerRequest (default 20), which BullMQ requires to be null.
problems.solution: Set maxRetriesPerRequest: null in the Redis connection options passed to BullMQ.
problems.code: const connection = { host: 'localhost', port: 6379, maxRetriesPerRequest: null };

further_reading.title: Background Jobs Best Practices
further_reading.note: Official BullMQ patterns and best practices

8.3.2.intuition: RabbitMQ is like a postal sorting office. Letters (messages) arrive and go to a sorting desk (exchange). Direct exchange is sorting by exact zip code. Fanout is making photocopies and sending to every branch. Topic exchange is sorting by partial address patterns — all mail for '1xx' streets goes to the north office.

exercises.title: Multi-Service Notification System
exercises.description: Build a fanout exchange that broadcasts user events to email, SMS, and push notification services. Each service has its own queue and consumer.
exercises.hint: Create a fanout exchange with three bound queues. Each consumer processes independently. Use Docker to run RabbitMQ locally.

exercises.title: Topic-Based Log Router
exercises.description: Implement a logging system using a topic exchange. Route logs based on `<service>.<level>` patterns. Error logs go to an alerting queue, all logs go to storage, and specific service logs go to their dashboard.
exercises.hint: Bind queues with patterns: `*.error` for alerts, `#` for storage, `auth.*` for auth dashboard.

exercises.title: Dead Letter Queue Handler
exercises.description: Set up a queue with a dead letter exchange. Implement a consumer that rejects messages after 3 processing attempts. Build a dead letter processor that logs failed messages and alerts operators.
exercises.hint: Track attempt count in message headers. Use `channel.nack(msg, false, false)` to send to DLQ after max attempts.

problems.problem: Messages being processed multiple times
problems.cause: Consumer processes the message but crashes before calling `channel.ack()`. RabbitMQ redelivers the unacknowledged message.
problems.solution: Make message processing idempotent (safe to run multiple times). Use unique message IDs and track processed IDs in the database. Check `msg.fields.redelivered` flag.

problems.problem: One consumer gets all messages while others sit idle
problems.cause: Prefetch count is not set, so RabbitMQ dispatches all messages to the first consumer in round-robin.
problems.solution: Set `channel.prefetch(1)` or an appropriate number. This ensures RabbitMQ only sends N unacknowledged messages to each consumer.
problems.code: channel.prefetch(10); // Each consumer gets max 10 unacked messages

problems.problem: Messages lost after RabbitMQ restart
problems.cause: Queue and/or messages were not configured as durable/persistent.
problems.solution: Declare queues with `{ durable: true }` and publish messages with `{ persistent: true }`. Also enable publisher confirms for guaranteed delivery.

further_reading.title: RabbitMQ Tutorials
further_reading.note: Official tutorials covering all exchange types

8.3.3.intuition: Think of a mail delivery system: retries are the mail carrier trying again tomorrow when no one is home. The dead letter office (DLQ) is where undeliverable mail goes after all attempts. Idempotency is like having a signature log — delivering the same package twice does not result in two deliveries because the recipient already signed for it.

exercises.title: Idempotent Payment Processor
exercises.description: Build a BullMQ worker that processes payments idempotently. Use Redis to track processed payments and ensure that retries never double-charge customers.
exercises.hint: Generate an idempotency key from orderId + amount. Use Redis SET NX to check/set processed state atomically.

exercises.title: Error Classification System
exercises.description: Create a worker that classifies errors into retryable (network, timeout, 5xx) and permanent (validation, 4xx). Retryable errors use exponential backoff; permanent errors go directly to DLQ.
exercises.hint: Create custom error classes. In the worker's failed event handler, check error type and move permanent errors to DLQ.

exercises.title: DLQ Dashboard and Replay
exercises.description: Build an admin API that lists DLQ messages, shows failure reasons, and allows replaying (re-enqueuing) selected messages back to the main queue.
exercises.hint: Store DLQ messages in a BullMQ queue. Admin endpoint reads jobs and can add them back to the main queue with fresh attempts.

problems.problem: Customers being charged multiple times
problems.cause: Payment processing job retried after timeout, but the original payment actually succeeded. The retry creates a second charge.
problems.solution: Implement idempotency using a unique key per payment (orderId). Check if the payment was already processed before charging. Use Stripe's built-in idempotency_key parameter.

problems.problem: DLQ growing indefinitely with no alerts
problems.cause: Dead letter queue is configured but no one monitors it. Failed messages accumulate silently.
problems.solution: Set up monitoring on DLQ depth. Alert when count exceeds threshold. Create a scheduled job that checks DLQ size and sends alerts.

further_reading.title: Exponential Backoff and Jitter
further_reading.note: AWS blog on optimal retry strategies

further_reading.title: Idempotency Patterns
further_reading.note: Stripe's approach to idempotent API requests

further_reading.title: Designing Reliable Systems
further_reading.note: Google SRE book chapter on handling overload

8.3.4.intuition: BullMQ scheduled jobs are like a shared office calendar that multiple employees can see. Instead of each employee setting their own alarm (cron), they all check the same calendar (Redis). Only one person handles each meeting (job), and if they are sick (crash), someone else picks it up.

exercises.title: Daily Cleanup Scheduler
exercises.description: Implement a BullMQ scheduled job that runs daily at 2 AM to clean up expired sessions, tokens, and temporary files. Log the number of cleaned items and track metrics.
exercises.hint: Use repeat pattern '0 2 * * *'. Return cleanup counts from the worker. Use removeOnComplete to keep history for 7 days.

exercises.title: Rate-Limited API Sync
exercises.description: Create a scheduled task that syncs data from an external API every 15 minutes. Implement rate limiting to avoid hitting the external API's rate limits.
exercises.hint: Use repeat every: 900000 (15 min). Use the worker's limiter option to control request rate. Handle 429 responses with delayed retry.

exercises.title: Schedule Management API
exercises.description: Build an admin API that lists all scheduled tasks, allows pausing/resuming specific schedules, and shows execution history.
exercises.hint: Use getRepeatableJobs() to list, removeRepeatableByKey() to pause (then re-add to resume). Use getCompleted() and getFailed() for history.

problems.problem: Scheduled job runs multiple times simultaneously
problems.cause: The job's execution time exceeds the repeat interval, so a new instance starts while the previous one is still running.
problems.solution: Use a distributed lock (Redlock) to ensure only one instance runs at a time. Or increase the interval to exceed the maximum expected execution time.

problems.problem: Duplicate repeatable jobs after application restart
problems.cause: The application adds repeatable jobs on startup without checking if they already exist, creating multiple schedules for the same task.
problems.solution: Use a unique jobId for each repeatable job. Or call getRepeatableJobs() and removeRepeatableByKey() before adding new ones.

further_reading.title: Cron Expression Generator
further_reading.note: Visual cron expression builder and validator

8.4.1.intuition: Database replication is like a professor (primary) giving a lecture that is live-streamed to overflow rooms (replicas). Students in overflow rooms see the same content with a slight delay. If students have questions (writes), they must go to the main lecture hall. If the professor is sick, a teaching assistant from an overflow room can take over.

exercises.title: Read/Write Splitting Service
exercises.description: Build an Express service that routes read queries to a PostgreSQL replica and write queries to the primary. Implement read-after-write consistency for the current user session.
exercises.hint: Create two Prisma clients. Track recent writers in Redis with a 5-second TTL. Route their reads to primary.

exercises.title: Replication Lag Monitor
exercises.description: Create a monitoring endpoint that checks replication lag and returns health status. Alert when lag exceeds 5 seconds.
exercises.hint: Use pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() on the replica. Calculate difference from now(). Return 503 if lag is too high.

exercises.title: Failover Simulation
exercises.description: Using Docker Compose, set up a primary + replica. Stop the primary, promote the replica to primary, and update your application's connection to use the new primary.
exercises.hint: Use pg_promote() or create a trigger file to promote the replica. Update environment variables and restart the app.

problems.problem: User creates a record but cannot see it immediately
problems.cause: The write goes to the primary, but the subsequent read goes to a replica that has not yet received the replication stream.
problems.solution: Implement read-after-write consistency: after a write, route that user's reads to the primary for a few seconds.
problems.symptoms: Users report 'missing data' that appears after refreshing a few seconds later.

problems.problem: Replica falls far behind primary
problems.cause: Replica is under heavy read load, has insufficient resources, or network between primary and replica is slow.
problems.solution: Monitor replication lag continuously. Add more replicas to distribute read load. Ensure replica has similar hardware to primary. Check for long-running queries on the replica that block WAL replay.

further_reading.title: Patroni - HA PostgreSQL
further_reading.note: Automatic failover for PostgreSQL

8.4.2.intuition: Sharding is like splitting a library's book collection across multiple buildings. Hash sharding assigns books by a formula (author name hash) — evenly distributed but you cannot browse alphabetically. Range sharding puts A-H in Building 1, I-P in Building 2, Q-Z in Building 3 — easy to browse a range but some letters have more books. Directory sharding has a central catalog telling you which building has which book — flexible but you always need to check the catalog first.

exercises.title: Hash Shard Router
exercises.description: Implement a hash-based shard router that distributes users across 3 PostgreSQL databases. Include methods for get, create, update, and delete operations.
exercises.hint: Use MD5 hash of the user ID modulo shard count. Create 3 Prisma clients pointing to different databases.

exercises.title: Cross-Shard Search
exercises.description: Build a search endpoint that queries all shards in parallel, merges results, and returns a paginated response with correct global ordering.
exercises.hint: Use Promise.all to query shards in parallel. Merge with Array.flat(), sort, then slice for pagination.

exercises.title: Shard Rebalancing
exercises.description: Implement a directory-based shard router with a rebalancing function that moves entities from overloaded shards to underloaded ones.
exercises.hint: Track entity count per shard in Redis. Move entities one at a time: copy data, update directory, delete from source.

problems.problem: One shard receiving significantly more traffic than others
problems.cause: The shard key has low cardinality or skewed distribution (e.g., sharding by country where 80% of users are in one country).
problems.solution: Choose a high-cardinality shard key like user_id. Use consistent hashing for better distribution. Monitor per-shard metrics and rebalance if needed.

problems.problem: Adding a new shard requires migrating massive amounts of data
problems.cause: Hash-based sharding with simple modulo: changing shard count (N to N+1) changes the shard assignment for most keys.
problems.solution: Use consistent hashing to minimize key redistribution. Or use directory-based sharding where you can assign new entities to new shards without moving existing data.

problems.problem: Cross-shard queries are extremely slow
problems.cause: Queries that need data from multiple shards require scatter-gather: querying all shards and merging results in the application.
problems.solution: Design the shard key so most queries only hit one shard. Denormalize data to avoid cross-shard joins. Use a search index (Elasticsearch) for cross-shard search queries.

further_reading.title: Sharding - MongoDB Manual
further_reading.note: MongoDB's approach applies conceptually to any database

further_reading.title: Vitess - MySQL Sharding
further_reading.note: YouTube's database sharding solution

8.4.3.body:
# Connection Pooling with PgBouncer

## Introduction

Every database query requires a connection. Creating a new PostgreSQL connection involves TCP handshake, TLS negotiation, authentication, and memory allocation — taking 50-100ms and consuming ~10MB of memory per connection. Connection pooling reuses existing connections, dramatically improving performance and allowing your application to handle far more concurrent requests than available database connections.

## The Problem

```
Without Pooling:
100 concurrent requests → 100 DB connections → DB overwhelmed

PostgreSQL default max_connections = 100
Each connection: ~10MB RAM
100 connections: ~1GB RAM just for connections

With Pooling:
100 concurrent requests → 20 DB connections (pooled) → DB handles easily
```

## Node.js Built-In Connection Pooling

Prisma and most Node.js database drivers include built-in connection pooling:

```javascript
// Prisma connection pool configuration
// DATABASE_URL="postgresql://user:pass@host:5432/db?connection_limit=20&pool_timeout=10"

import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

const prisma = new PrismaClient({
  datasources: {
    db: {
      url: process.env.DATABASE_URL,
      // Connection pool settings are in the URL
    },
  },
  log: ['query', 'info', 'warn', 'error'],
});

// Knex.js connection pool
import knex from 'knex';

const db = knex({
  client: 'pg',
  connection: process.env.DATABASE_URL,
  pool: {
    min: 2,           // Minimum connections to maintain
    max: 20,          // Maximum connections
    idleTimeoutMillis: 30000,    // Close idle connections after 30s
    createTimeoutMillis: 5000,   // Max time to create new connection
    acquireTimeoutMillis: 10000, // Max time to acquire from pool
    reapIntervalMillis: 1000,    // How often to check for idle connections
  },
});

// pg (node-postgres) pool
import pg from 'pg';

const pool = new pg.Pool({
  connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL,
  max: 20,
  idleTimeoutMillis: 30000,
  connectionTimeoutMillis: 5000,
});

// Monitor pool events
pool.on('connect', () => console.log('New connection created'));
pool.on('acquire', () => console.log('Connection acquired'));
pool.on('remove', () => console.log('Connection removed'));
pool.on('error', (err) => console.error('Pool error:', err));
```

## PgBouncer: External Connection Pooler

PgBouncer sits between your application and PostgreSQL, managing a shared pool of connections:

```
Without PgBouncer:
┌──────────┐  20 conn   ┌────────────┐
│ App 1    │────────────>│            │
└──────────┘             │            │
┌──────────┐  20 conn   │ PostgreSQL │  60 total connections
│ App 2    │────────────>│            │
└──────────┘             │            │
┌──────────┐  20 conn   │            │
│ App 3    │────────────>│            │
└──────────┘             └────────────┘

With PgBouncer:
┌──────────┐             ┌───────────┐             ┌────────────┐
│ App 1    │──20 conn──> │           │             │            │
└──────────┘             │           │  20 conn    │            │
┌──────────┐             │ PgBouncer │────────────>│ PostgreSQL │
│ App 2    │──20 conn──> │           │  (shared)   │            │
└──────────┘             │           │             │            │
┌──────────┐             │           │             │            │
│ App 3    │──20 conn──> │           │             │            │
└──────────┘             └───────────┘             └────────────┘
                          60 app connections        20 DB connections
```

### PgBouncer Modes

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Mode               │ Connection Reuse   │ Feature Support    │
├────────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
│ session            │ Per client session │ Full SQL support   │
│ (default)          │ (released on       │ Prepared stmts OK  │
│                    │  disconnect)       │                    │
├────────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
│ transaction        │ Per transaction    │ Most SQL           │
│ (recommended)      │ (released after    │ No prepared stmts  │
│                    │  COMMIT/ROLLBACK)  │ across txns        │
├────────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
│ statement          │ Per statement      │ No multi-statement │
│ (most aggressive)  │ (released after    │ No transactions    │
│                    │  each query)       │                    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### PgBouncer Configuration

```ini
; /etc/pgbouncer/pgbouncer.ini
[databases]
myapp = host=postgres-primary port=5432 dbname=myapp

[pgbouncer]
listen_port = 6432
listen_addr = 0.0.0.0

; Pool mode
pool_mode = transaction

; Pool sizing
default_pool_size = 20
min_pool_size = 5
max_client_conn = 200        ; Max frontend connections
max_db_connections = 50      ; Max backend connections per database

; Timeouts
server_idle_timeout = 600    ; Close idle backend connections after 10 min
client_idle_timeout = 0      ; No timeout for idle frontend connections
query_timeout = 30           ; Kill queries running longer than 30s
query_wait_timeout = 120     ; Max time to wait for a connection from pool

; Authentication
auth_type = md5
auth_file = /etc/pgbouncer/userlist.txt

; Logging
log_connections = 1
log_disconnections = 1
log_pooler_errors = 1
stats_period = 60
```

### Docker Compose with PgBouncer

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp
      POSTGRES_USER: app
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    # Only accessible via PgBouncer, not directly
    expose:
      - "5432"

  pgbouncer:
    image: edoburu/pgbouncer
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgres://app:secret@postgres:5432/myapp
      POOL_MODE: transaction
      DEFAULT_POOL_SIZE: 20
      MAX_CLIENT_CONN: 200
      MAX_DB_CONNECTIONS: 50
    ports:
      - "6432:5432"
    depends_on:
      - postgres

  app:
    build: .
    environment:
      # App connects to PgBouncer, NOT directly to PostgreSQL
      DATABASE_URL: postgres://app:secret@pgbouncer:5432/myapp
    depends_on:
      - pgbouncer

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

### Connecting Node.js to PgBouncer

```javascript
// Prisma with PgBouncer
// schema.prisma - add pgbouncer=true to enable compatible mode
// DATABASE_URL="postgresql://app:secret@pgbouncer:6432/myapp?pgbouncer=true&connection_limit=10"

const prisma = new PrismaClient();

// Knex with PgBouncer
const db = knex({
  client: 'pg',
  connection: 'postgresql://app:secret@pgbouncer:6432/myapp',
  pool: {
    min: 2,
    max: 10, // Per-instance limit; PgBouncer manages the real pool
  },
});
```

## Monitoring Connection Pools

```javascript
// Monitor Prisma connection pool
const prisma = new PrismaClient({
  log: [
    { emit: 'event', level: 'query' },
  ],
});

prisma.$on('query', (e) => {
  if (e.duration > 1000) {
    console.warn(`Slow query (${e.duration}ms): ${e.query}`);
  }
});

// Monitor pg Pool
app.get('/admin/pool-stats', async (req, res) => {
  res.json({
    totalCount: pool.totalCount,     // Total connections in pool
    idleCount: pool.idleCount,       // Idle connections
    waitingCount: pool.waitingCount, // Clients waiting for connection
  });
});

// PgBouncer stats via SQL
async function getPgBouncerStats() {
  const client = new pg.Client('postgresql://pgbouncer:6432/pgbouncer');
  await client.connect();

  const pools = await client.query('SHOW POOLS');
  const stats = await client.query('SHOW STATS');
  const clients = await client.query('SHOW CLIENTS');

  await client.end();
  return { pools: pools.rows, stats: stats.rows, clients: clients.rows };
}
```

## Pool Sizing Guidelines

```
Rule of thumb for PostgreSQL:
  max_connections = (CPU cores * 2) + effective_io_concurrency

For a 4-core server: max_connections = (4 * 2) + 2 = 10

PgBouncer allows many app connections mapped to fewer DB connections:
  App instances: 10 (each with pool of 20)
  PgBouncer max_client_conn: 200
  PgBouncer max_db_connections: 20
  PostgreSQL max_connections: 25 (20 + overhead)
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Too many connections**: More connections != better performance. PostgreSQL performs worse with too many active connections due to context switching.
2. **Prepared statements with PgBouncer transaction mode**: PgBouncer in transaction mode doesn't support prepared statements across transactions. Use `pgbouncer=true` in Prisma.
3. **Connection leak**: Not releasing connections back to the pool (forgetting to call `client.release()` with pg). Use try/finally.
4. **Pool timeout too short**: Under load, pool exhaustion causes query failures. Set appropriate acquire timeout.

## Summary

Connection pooling is essential for production Node.js applications. Application-level pooling (Prisma, Knex, pg.Pool) manages connections within a single process. PgBouncer provides external pooling that works across multiple application instances, dramatically reducing the number of PostgreSQL connections needed. Use transaction mode for the best connection reuse, and size your pool based on CPU cores rather than expected concurrent requests.
8.4.3.intuition: Connection pooling is like a car-sharing service. Instead of everyone buying their own car (connection) that sits idle 90% of the time, a shared pool of cars serves everyone. PgBouncer is like adding a parking garage (external pooler) between the apartment building (app) and the highway (database) — residents don't each need a parking spot at the highway.

exercises.title: Pool Monitoring Dashboard
exercises.description: Create an Express endpoint that displays real-time connection pool statistics: total, idle, active, and waiting connections. Add warnings when pool utilization exceeds 80%.
exercises.hint: Use pg.Pool properties: totalCount, idleCount, waitingCount. Active = total - idle.

exercises.title: PgBouncer Setup
exercises.description: Set up PgBouncer with Docker Compose in transaction mode. Connect a Node.js app through PgBouncer and verify connection reuse by checking pg_stat_activity.
exercises.hint: Use the edoburu/pgbouncer Docker image. Query pg_stat_activity to see active connections. They should be much fewer than app connections.

exercises.title: Connection Leak Detector
exercises.description: Build a wrapper around pg.Pool that detects connection leaks: connections checked out for more than 30 seconds without being released.
exercises.hint: Track checkout time per connection. Use setInterval to check for connections held longer than the threshold.

problems.problem: Application hangs under load with 'connection pool timeout' errors
problems.cause: All connections in the pool are in use and new queries cannot acquire a connection within the timeout period.
problems.solution: Increase pool size, optimize slow queries that hold connections too long, or add PgBouncer. Check for connection leaks (unreleased connections).
problems.symptoms: Application works fine under low load but hangs or returns errors under high load.

problems.problem: Prisma errors with PgBouncer: 'prepared statement does not exist'
problems.cause: PgBouncer in transaction mode doesn't support named prepared statements across transactions, which Prisma uses by default.
problems.solution: Add `?pgbouncer=true` to your DATABASE_URL. This tells Prisma to use protocol-level prepared statements compatible with PgBouncer.

further_reading.title: Sizing Your Connection Pool
further_reading.note: HikariCP article but principles apply universally

8.4.4.intuition: Partitioning is like organizing a filing cabinet. Instead of one giant drawer with millions of papers (one huge table), you split them into drawers by year (range partitioning). When looking for a 2024 invoice, you only search the 2024 drawer, ignoring all others. This is partition pruning — skipping irrelevant drawers entirely.

exercises.title: Partitioned Orders Table
exercises.description: Create a range-partitioned orders table by month. Insert 1M records across 12 months. Compare query performance with and without the partition key in the WHERE clause.
exercises.hint: Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to compare. Queries with created_at filter should show 'Partition Pruning' in the plan.

exercises.title: Auto-Partition Manager
exercises.description: Build a BullMQ scheduled job that automatically creates partitions for the next 3 months ahead of time, preventing insert failures when new months begin.
exercises.hint: Run monthly. Check which partitions exist with pg_catalog queries. Create missing future partitions.

exercises.title: Materialized View Dashboard
exercises.description: Create materialized views for daily order statistics and hourly active users. Build an API that serves these precomputed stats and schedule regular refreshes.
exercises.hint: Use REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY for zero-downtime refresh. Expose via GET /admin/stats endpoint.

problems.problem: Inserts failing with 'no partition of relation found for row'
problems.cause: A new row's partition key value doesn't match any existing partition range (e.g., new year started but partition wasn't created).
problems.solution: Create partitions ahead of time using a scheduled job. Add a default partition as a safety net.
problems.code: CREATE TABLE orders_default PARTITION OF orders DEFAULT;

problems.problem: Partitioned queries not faster than unpartitioned
problems.cause: Queries don't include the partition key in WHERE clause, so PostgreSQL scans all partitions (no pruning).
problems.solution: Always include the partition key (e.g., created_at for time-partitioned tables) in WHERE clauses. Check with EXPLAIN that partition pruning is happening.

further_reading.title: Use The Index, Luke!
further_reading.note: Free online book about SQL indexing and query optimization

8.5.1.intuition: The cluster module is like a restaurant manager (primary process) who hires multiple chefs (workers). Each chef has their own workstation but they all serve the same dining room (port). If a chef calls in sick, the manager hires a replacement. During a menu change, the manager trains new chefs one at a time while the old ones finish their current orders (zero-downtime restart).

exercises.title: Clustered Express Server
exercises.description: Build an Express app using the cluster module that spawns workers equal to CPU count. Add automatic worker replacement on crash and a metrics endpoint on the primary process.
exercises.hint: Use cluster.on('exit') to detect crashes and cluster.fork() to replace. Track worker PIDs and states.

exercises.title: Zero-Downtime Restart
exercises.description: Implement zero-downtime restart triggered by SIGUSR2 signal. New workers start before old ones shut down, ensuring no dropped requests.
exercises.hint: Start new worker, wait for 'listening' event, then disconnect old worker. Use server.close() for graceful shutdown.

exercises.title: PM2 Production Deployment
exercises.description: Configure an ecosystem.config.js for a NestJS application with cluster mode, memory limits, graceful shutdown, and log management. Test zero-downtime reload.
exercises.hint: Set exec_mode: 'cluster', instances: 'max', wait_ready: true. Use process.send('ready') in your app.

problems.problem: Only one worker receiving all requests
problems.cause: On some platforms (notably Windows), the OS scheduling may favor one worker. Linux distributes connections more evenly.
problems.solution: On Linux, the default round-robin scheduling works well. On other platforms, consider using a reverse proxy (Nginx) for more even distribution.

problems.problem: Session data lost between requests
problems.cause: Requests are routed to different workers, and sessions are stored in worker memory.
problems.solution: Use Redis-backed sessions (connect-redis) or stateless JWT authentication. Never store sessions in worker memory.

problems.problem: Memory usage multiplied by worker count
problems.cause: Each worker is a full Node.js process with its own V8 heap. 8 workers each using 200MB = 1.6GB total.
problems.solution: Monitor per-worker memory usage. Set max_memory_restart in PM2. Optimize application memory usage before adding workers.

8.5.2.intuition: Worker threads are like hiring assistant cooks (threads) in the same kitchen (process). They share the pantry (memory) but work at their own stations. The head chef (main thread) delegates the heavy chopping (CPU work) to assistants, staying free to take new orders (handle requests). A worker pool is pre-hiring a fixed team rather than calling temp workers for each dish.

exercises.title: Worker Pool Implementation
exercises.description: Build a worker thread pool that manages a fixed number of workers. Support task queuing when all workers are busy and automatic worker replacement on crashes.
exercises.hint: Track free/busy workers. Queue tasks as Promises. On worker message, resolve the pending task and pick next from queue.

exercises.title: Password Hashing Service
exercises.description: Create an Express endpoint that hashes passwords using bcrypt in a worker thread. Benchmark the throughput compared to hashing on the main thread.
exercises.hint: Use a worker pool with 4 threads. Compare with autocannon: main thread version will block under load.

exercises.title: Parallel Data Processing
exercises.description: Split a large array into chunks and process each chunk in a separate worker thread using SharedArrayBuffer. Combine results on the main thread.
exercises.hint: Create a SharedArrayBuffer, divide into chunks by index range, spawn workers for each chunk, aggregate results.

problems.problem: Worker thread creation is slow under load
problems.cause: Creating a new Worker for each request has ~50ms overhead for V8 initialization.
problems.solution: Use a worker pool (Piscina or custom). Pre-create workers at startup and reuse them for subsequent tasks.

problems.problem: Large data transfer between main and worker is slow
problems.cause: postMessage serializes (structured clone) the data, which is slow for large objects.
problems.solution: Use SharedArrayBuffer for large numeric data. Use transferable objects (ArrayBuffer, MessagePort) to transfer ownership without copying.

problems.problem: Worker thread causes main thread to hang
problems.cause: Using Atomics.wait() on the main thread blocks the event loop.
problems.solution: Never use Atomics.wait() on the main thread. Use Atomics.waitAsync() instead, or use message passing for synchronization.

8.5.3.intuition: Concurrency limits are like a restaurant that only seats 50 people at a time. Without limits, 500 people rush in and nobody gets served (system overload). With limits, customers wait in a queue and are seated as tables free up. Backpressure is when the kitchen (consumer) tells the waiter (producer) to slow down taking orders because they're falling behind.

exercises.title: Concurrent API Caller
exercises.description: Build a function that fetches data from 1000 URLs with a concurrency limit of 20. Track progress and handle individual request failures without stopping the batch.
exercises.hint: Use p-limit(20). Wrap each fetch in try/catch. Track success/fail counts.

exercises.title: Backpressure-Aware Stream Pipeline
exercises.description: Create a stream pipeline that reads from a database, transforms records, and writes to a file. Ensure backpressure works correctly — the database read pauses when the file write is slow.
exercises.hint: Use pipeline() from stream/promises. Set objectMode and appropriate highWaterMark. Monitor buffer sizes.

exercises.title: Load Shedding Middleware
exercises.description: Implement Express middleware that tracks concurrent requests and returns 503 when above threshold. Add a queue with max size for requests that wait.
exercises.hint: Track active count. If above limit and queue full, return 503 with Retry-After header.

problems.problem: Application runs out of memory processing large datasets
problems.cause: Using Promise.all or Array.map to process millions of records loads everything into memory simultaneously.
problems.solution: Use streaming with backpressure or batch processing with concurrency limits. Process chunks of 100-1000 records at a time.

problems.problem: External API returning 429 (rate limit) errors
problems.cause: Application sends too many requests too quickly to an external API.
problems.solution: Implement a rate limiter (token bucket) for outbound requests. Respect Retry-After headers. Use exponential backoff on 429 responses.

problems.problem: Event loop lag increasing under load
problems.cause: Too many concurrent operations competing for the event loop, or large data being serialized/deserialized synchronously.
problems.solution: Add concurrency limits. Use worker threads for CPU-bound work. Monitor event loop lag and shed load when lag exceeds threshold.

further_reading.title: p-limit
further_reading.note: Simple promise concurrency limiter

further_reading.title: Load Shedding in Node.js
further_reading.note: While Go-focused, the load shedding concepts apply universally

8.6.1.body:
# Service Decomposition and Bounded Contexts

## Introduction

Microservices architecture decomposes a monolithic application into small, independently deployable services. The key challenge is deciding where to draw service boundaries. Domain-Driven Design (DDD) provides the answer through **bounded contexts** — well-defined areas of the business domain where a particular model applies.

## Monolith vs Microservices

```
Monolith:                           Microservices:
┌─────────────────────┐             ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐
│    One Codebase     │             │  User  │ │ Order  │ │Payment │
│                     │             │Service │ │Service │ │Service │
│ Users  Orders       │             └───┬────┘ └───┬────┘ └───┬────┘
│ Payments Shipping   │      =>         │          │          │
│ Notifications       │             ┌───┴────┐ ┌───┴────┐ ┌───┴────┐
│                     │             │User DB │ │Order DB│ │Pay DB  │
│ One Database        │             └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘
└─────────────────────┘
```

## Bounded Contexts

A bounded context is a boundary within which a domain model is defined and consistent. The same real-world concept may have different representations in different contexts.

```
E-Commerce Example:

┌─ User Context ─────────┐  ┌─ Order Context ──────────┐
│                         │  │                          │
│ User {                  │  │ Customer {               │
│   id, name, email,      │  │   id, name,              │
│   password, preferences,│  │   shippingAddress,        │
│   loginHistory          │  │   billingAddress          │
│ }                       │  │ }                        │
│                         │  │                          │
│ Cares about: auth,      │  │ Cares about: who ordered │
│ profile, preferences    │  │ and where to ship        │
└─────────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────────┘

Same person, different models!
User Context doesn't need shippingAddress.
Order Context doesn't need password or loginHistory.
```

## Decomposition Strategies

### 1. Decompose by Business Capability

```
Business Capabilities → Services:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Business Capability      │ Service                   │
├──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
│ User Management          │ User Service              │
│ Product Catalog          │ Catalog Service           │
│ Order Management         │ Order Service             │
│ Payment Processing       │ Payment Service           │
│ Shipping & Delivery      │ Shipping Service          │
│ Notifications            │ Notification Service      │
│ Analytics & Reporting    │ Analytics Service         │
│ Inventory Management     │ Inventory Service         │
└──────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────┘
```

### 2. Decompose by Subdomain

```
DDD Subdomain Classification:

Core Domain (competitive advantage):
  - Product Recommendation Engine
  - Pricing Strategy
  → Most investment, custom development

Supporting Domain (necessary but not differentiating):
  - Inventory Management
  - Shipping Coordination
  → Build or buy, moderate investment

Generic Domain (common across industries):
  - User Authentication
  - Payment Processing
  - Email Sending
  → Buy/use SaaS (Auth0, Stripe, SendGrid)
```

## Service Communication

```
Synchronous (Request-Response):
  ┌──────┐  HTTP/gRPC  ┌──────┐
  │Svc A │────────────>│Svc B │
  │      │<────────────│      │
  └──────┘  Response    └──────┘

Asynchronous (Event-Driven):
  ┌──────┐   Event    ┌──────────┐   Event   ┌──────┐
  │Svc A │──────────>│  Message  │─────────>│Svc B │
  │      │           │  Broker   │          │      │
  └──────┘           │(Kafka/MQ) │          └──────┘
                     └──────────┘
```

```javascript
// Synchronous: Order Service calls User Service
class OrderService {
  constructor(userServiceClient) {
    this.userServiceClient = userServiceClient;
  }

  async createOrder(userId, items) {
    // Sync call to validate user
    const user = await this.userServiceClient.getUser(userId);
    if (!user) throw new Error('User not found');

    const order = await this.orderRepo.create({
      customerId: userId,
      customerName: user.name,
      items,
      status: 'PENDING',
    });

    // Async: publish event for other services
    await this.eventBus.publish('order.created', {
      orderId: order.id,
      customerId: userId,
      items,
      total: order.total,
    });

    return order;
  }
}

// HTTP client for service-to-service calls
class UserServiceClient {
  constructor(baseUrl) {
    this.baseUrl = baseUrl;
  }

  async getUser(userId) {
    const response = await fetch(`${this.baseUrl}/api/users/${userId}`, {
      headers: { 'X-Service': 'order-service' },
      signal: AbortSignal.timeout(3000), // 3s timeout
    });

    if (response.status === 404) return null;
    if (!response.ok) throw new Error(`User service error: ${response.status}`);
    return response.json();
  }
}
```

## Data Ownership

Each service owns its data. No shared databases.

```
WRONG: Shared Database
┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐
│Svc A │  │Svc B │  │Svc C │
└──┬───┘  └──┬───┘  └──┬───┘
   │         │         │
   └─────────┼─────────┘
             ▼
    ┌────────────────┐
    │ Shared Database │  ← Tight coupling!
    └────────────────┘

RIGHT: Database Per Service
┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐
│Svc A │  │Svc B │  │Svc C │
└──┬───┘  └──┬───┘  └──┬───┘
   │         │         │
   ▼         ▼         ▼
┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐
│DB A  │  │DB B  │  │DB C  │
└──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘
```

```javascript
// Data duplication via events (eventual consistency)

// User Service publishes events when user data changes
class UserService {
  async updateProfile(userId, data) {
    const user = await this.userRepo.update(userId, data);

    // Publish event for other services to update their copies
    await this.eventBus.publish('user.profile.updated', {
      userId: user.id,
      name: user.name,
      email: user.email,
      // Only fields other services need
    });

    return user;
  }
}

// Order Service maintains its own copy of customer data
class OrderEventHandler {
  async handleUserUpdated(event) {
    // Update the local customer record
    await this.customerRepo.upsert({
      where: { externalUserId: event.userId },
      update: { name: event.name, email: event.email },
      create: {
        externalUserId: event.userId,
        name: event.name,
        email: event.email,
      },
    });
  }
}
```

## NestJS Monorepo for Microservices

```
project/
├── apps/
│   ├── user-service/
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── user.module.ts
│   │   │   ├── user.controller.ts
│   │   │   ├── user.service.ts
│   │   │   └── main.ts
│   │   └── Dockerfile
│   ├── order-service/
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── order.module.ts
│   │   │   ├── order.controller.ts
│   │   │   └── main.ts
│   │   └── Dockerfile
│   └── api-gateway/
│       ├── src/
│       │   └── main.ts
│       └── Dockerfile
├── libs/
│   └── shared/
│       ├── dto/
│       ├── interfaces/
│       └── events/
└── docker-compose.yml
```

## When NOT to Use Microservices

```
Start with a monolith when:
  - Small team (< 10 developers)
  - Early-stage product (requirements unclear)
  - Simple domain
  - No need for independent deployment
  - No polyglot requirement

Move to microservices when:
  - Teams need to deploy independently
  - Different scaling requirements per feature
  - Clear domain boundaries have emerged
  - Organizational growth demands it
  - Monolith deployments are too risky/slow
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Distributed monolith**: Services that must be deployed together and share databases — all the complexity of microservices with none of the benefits.
2. **Too many services too early**: Start with a well-structured monolith. Extract services as boundaries become clear.
3. **Synchronous chains**: Service A calls B calls C calls D — latency compounds and one failure cascades.
4. **Shared database**: Two services using the same database table creates tight coupling.

## Summary

Service decomposition requires identifying bounded contexts — areas of the business with distinct models and responsibilities. Each service owns its data, communicates via APIs or events, and can be deployed independently. Use DDD to identify core, supporting, and generic domains. Start with a modular monolith and extract services when organizational and technical pressure demands it.
8.6.1.intuition: Bounded contexts are like departments in a company. HR, Engineering, and Finance all have a concept of 'employee' but track different attributes (salary history vs. GitHub commits vs. tax withholdings). Each department owns its own records and communicates with others through memos (events) or phone calls (APIs), not by reaching into each other's filing cabinets (shared database).

exercises.title: Domain Decomposition
exercises.description: Take an e-commerce monolith with users, products, orders, payments, shipping, and notifications. Identify bounded contexts, define service boundaries, and specify which data each service owns.
exercises.hint: Group by business capability. Identify which entities belong to which context. Note where the same concept (e.g., 'user') has different models in different contexts.

exercises.title: Service Communication Design
exercises.description: For an order creation flow involving User, Inventory, Payment, and Notification services, design both synchronous (validate user, check inventory) and asynchronous (payment, notification) communication patterns.
exercises.hint: Synchronous for queries that block the flow. Asynchronous for actions that can happen eventually. Draw a sequence diagram.

exercises.title: Event-Driven Data Sync
exercises.description: Implement event-based data synchronization between a User Service and Order Service. When a user updates their address, the Order Service updates its local customer record.
exercises.hint: User Service publishes 'user.updated' events. Order Service subscribes and updates its customer table.

problems.problem: Services are tightly coupled and must deploy together
problems.cause: Services share a database or have synchronous dependency chains that break when any service changes.
problems.solution: Each service must own its data. Use events for data propagation. Design APIs with backward compatibility (versioning).

problems.problem: Microservices slower than the monolith
problems.cause: What was a function call in the monolith is now a network call. Latency adds up across service chains.
problems.solution: Minimize synchronous service-to-service calls. Use async events where possible. Denormalize data to avoid cross-service queries. Add caching.

problems.problem: Difficult to trace requests across services
problems.cause: A single user request triggers calls across multiple services, making debugging difficult.
problems.solution: Implement distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry. Use correlation IDs that propagate across all service calls. Centralize logs.

further_reading.title: MonolithFirst - Martin Fowler
further_reading.note: Why you should start with a monolith

8.6.2.intuition: An API Gateway is like the front desk of a hotel. Guests (clients) don't wander the halls looking for housekeeping, room service, or concierge (individual services). They call the front desk, which routes their request to the right department. Service discovery is the hotel's internal phone directory that automatically updates when staff move to different extensions.

exercises.title: Express API Gateway
exercises.description: Build an API Gateway using Express and http-proxy-middleware that routes to 3 backend services. Add JWT authentication, rate limiting, and request logging as middleware.
exercises.hint: Use createProxyMiddleware for routing. Add auth middleware before proxy routes. Use express-rate-limit for rate limiting.

exercises.title: Service Registry with Redis
exercises.description: Implement a service registry where services register on startup, send heartbeats, and deregister on shutdown. Build a discovery client that finds healthy instances with round-robin selection.
exercises.hint: Use Redis hashes for instance storage. Track lastHeartbeat timestamps. Remove stale instances.

exercises.title: Response Aggregation
exercises.description: Build a /dashboard endpoint in the API Gateway that aggregates data from User, Order, and Product services in parallel, with timeout handling for individual service failures.
exercises.hint: Use Promise.allSettled to handle partial failures. Return available data with error flags for failed services.

problems.problem: API Gateway becomes a single point of failure
problems.cause: Running only one gateway instance means all traffic stops if it goes down.
problems.solution: Deploy multiple gateway instances behind a load balancer (Nginx, ALB). Make the gateway stateless so any instance can handle any request.

problems.problem: Service discovery returns stale instances
problems.cause: A service instance crashed without deregistering, and its heartbeat has not expired yet.
problems.solution: Use short heartbeat intervals (10-15s). Implement health check endpoints. Remove instances that miss 2-3 heartbeats.

further_reading.title: Kong API Gateway
further_reading.note: Production-grade API gateway

8.6.3.intuition: NestJS microservice transports are like different types of mail service. TCP is like a direct phone call — you wait for an answer. Redis pub/sub is like a bulletin board — you post a notice and whoever is interested reads it. RabbitMQ is like certified mail — guaranteed delivery with a receipt. Kafka is like a newspaper — published once, read by many subscribers at their own pace.

exercises.title: TCP Microservice Pair
exercises.description: Build two NestJS microservices communicating over TCP: a User Service and an Order Service. The Order Service calls the User Service to validate users before creating orders.
exercises.hint: Use ClientsModule.register with Transport.TCP. Use firstValueFrom to convert Observable to Promise.

exercises.title: Event-Driven with Redis
