exercises.description: Build a system where an Order Service emits events via Redis transport, and both a Notification Service and an Analytics Service consume them independently.
exercises.hint: Use @EventPattern in both consumers. Each service registers with a different consumer group.

exercises.title: Hybrid HTTP + Microservice
exercises.description: Create a NestJS service that exposes both REST endpoints for external clients and microservice message patterns for internal services. Use app.connectMicroservice for the TCP transport.
exercises.hint: Use NestFactory.create for HTTP, then connectMicroservice for TCP. Call startAllMicroservices before listen.

problems.problem: ClientProxy.send() hangs and never resolves
problems.cause: The target microservice is not running or not listening on the expected host:port.
problems.solution: Add timeout to requests. Implement health checks. Use `firstValueFrom` with `timeout` operator from rxjs.

problems.problem: Events being processed multiple times
problems.cause: Multiple instances of the same service are consuming from the same Redis/RabbitMQ channel without consumer groups.
problems.solution: Use consumer groups (Kafka) or exclusive queues (RabbitMQ). For Redis, use different channel names or implement idempotent handlers.

8.6.4.intuition: A saga is like planning a wedding involving multiple vendors. The choreography approach is like each vendor knowing what to do when they hear the previous vendor finished (florist delivers after venue confirms). The orchestration approach is like having a wedding planner who calls each vendor in order and handles cancellations if something goes wrong. If the caterer cancels last-minute, the planner cancels the venue and florist (compensating actions).

exercises.title: Orchestrated Order Saga
exercises.description: Implement a saga orchestrator for order processing: reserve inventory, charge payment, create shipment. Include compensating actions that run in reverse on failure.
exercises.hint: Define steps as objects with execute and compensate functions. Track completed steps. On failure, compensate in reverse order.

exercises.title: Choreography-Based Saga
exercises.description: Build 3 NestJS microservices that communicate via events for an order saga. Each service publishes success/failure events that other services react to.
exercises.hint: Use Redis or RabbitMQ transport. Each service has @EventPattern handlers for both success and failure events.

exercises.title: Saga Recovery
exercises.description: Implement persistent saga state in Redis. Build a recovery worker that detects stalled sagas (no progress for 5 minutes) and automatically triggers compensation.
exercises.hint: Store saga state with timestamps. A scheduled job checks for sagas stuck in non-terminal states past timeout.

problems.problem: Saga leaves data in inconsistent state after partial failure
problems.cause: Compensation action failed (e.g., refund API returned error), leaving the saga incomplete.
problems.solution: Retry compensating actions with exponential backoff. Log all compensation failures. Have a manual intervention process for unrecoverable cases. Send alerts to operations team.

problems.problem: Events processed out of order in choreography saga
problems.cause: Message broker doesn't guarantee ordering, so 'payment.charged' arrives before 'inventory.reserved'.
problems.solution: Each step should verify prerequisites before executing. Store saga state and only proceed when all required prior events have been received.

further_reading.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
further_reading.note: Chapter 9 covers distributed transactions and sagas

8.7.1.intuition: Event sourcing is like a bank ledger. Instead of overwriting your balance, every deposit and withdrawal is recorded as a line item. You can always recalculate the balance by replaying the ledger from the beginning, and you have a complete audit trail. CQRS is like having a separate account summary (read model) that is updated whenever a new line is added to the ledger, so you don't have to read the entire ledger every time you want to check your balance.

exercises.title: Event Store Implementation
exercises.description: Build an event store with PostgreSQL that supports appending events, reading events by stream, and optimistic concurrency control.
exercises.hint: Use a unique constraint on (stream_id, version). Check version before appending.

exercises.title: Order Aggregate with Event Sourcing
exercises.description: Implement an Order aggregate that handles create, pay, ship, and cancel commands. Rebuild state from events. Write tests that verify state after applying various event sequences.
exercises.hint: Separate command handling (business rules) from event application (state changes). Test by applying events and checking state.

exercises.title: CQRS Read Projection
exercises.description: Build a projection that materializes order events into a read-optimized PostgreSQL view. Create a query service that provides filtered, paginated order listings.
exercises.hint: Listen to events from the store. Switch on event type and upsert into the read model table.

problems.problem: Event replay takes too long for aggregates with many events
problems.cause: An aggregate with 10,000+ events requires replaying all of them to rebuild current state.
problems.solution: Implement periodic snapshots that cache aggregate state. On load, start from latest snapshot and replay only subsequent events.

problems.problem: Read model is stale (eventual consistency gap)
problems.cause: The projection process has not yet processed the latest events. Users see outdated data after a write.
problems.solution: For read-after-write scenarios, query the event store directly. Or return the expected state optimistically in the write response.

further_reading.title: Event Sourcing in Practice - Greg Young

8.7.2.intuition: Kafka is like a newspaper distribution system. The printing press (producer) publishes editions (messages) organized by section (topic). Each section has multiple delivery routes (partitions). Subscribers (consumer groups) each get their own copy of every edition. Unlike a mailbox where mail is removed after reading, Kafka keeps all editions in the archive for a configured retention period, allowing new subscribers to read from the beginning.

exercises.title: Kafka Producer-Consumer Setup
exercises.description: Set up Kafka with Docker Compose. Build a producer that sends order events and a consumer that processes them. Use message keys for partition affinity.
exercises.hint: Use docker-compose with confluentinc/cp-kafka image. Set message key to orderId for ordering guarantee per order.

exercises.title: Multi-Consumer Group Processing
exercises.description: Create two consumer groups consuming the same topic: one for order processing and one for analytics. Verify both groups independently receive all messages.
exercises.hint: Create two consumers with different groupId values. Both subscribe to the same topic.

exercises.title: Exactly-Once Processing
exercises.description: Implement idempotent message processing with manual offset commits. Ensure that even if a consumer restarts mid-batch, no messages are processed twice.
exercises.hint: Use autoCommit: false. Track processed message IDs in the database. Only commit offset after database write succeeds.

problems.problem: Messages for the same entity processed out of order
problems.cause: Messages are sent without a key, so they land on different partitions. Partitions are consumed independently.
problems.solution: Always set a message key that groups related messages (e.g., orderId, userId). Messages with the same key go to the same partition and are processed in order.

problems.problem: Consumer group constantly rebalancing
problems.cause: Consumer processing takes too long, exceeding the session timeout. Kafka thinks the consumer is dead.
problems.solution: Increase session.timeout.ms. Call heartbeat() during long processing. Reduce per-message processing time or increase partition count for more parallelism.

8.7.3.intuition: Domain events with EventEmitter are like a company-wide announcement system. When something important happens (new order placed), an announcement goes out over the PA system. Different departments (email, analytics, inventory) each hear the same announcement and take their own appropriate action, without the announcer needing to know who is listening or what they will do.

exercises.title: Domain Event Bus
exercises.description: Build a typed event bus with async emission, error isolation (one handler failure doesn't affect others), and event logging. Register handlers for order and user events.
exercises.hint: Use Promise.allSettled for async emission. Log failed handlers with event details.

exercises.title: Event-Driven Order System
exercises.description: Implement an order creation flow where the service emits events consumed by email, analytics, and inventory handlers. Each handler is independent and failure-isolated.
exercises.hint: OrderService emits 'order.created'. Three separate handler classes register for this event.

exercises.title: EventEmitter to Kafka Bridge
exercises.description: Create a bridge that automatically publishes selected in-process events to Kafka topics for cross-service consumption.
exercises.hint: Map event types to Kafka topics. On each event, serialize and send to the corresponding topic.

problems.problem: Unhandled error in event handler crashes the process
problems.cause: An async event handler throws an error that is not caught. Node.js EventEmitter propagates errors.
problems.solution: Always wrap event handlers in try/catch. Use emitAsync with Promise.allSettled. Add a global 'error' event handler on the emitter.

problems.problem: Memory leak warning: MaxListenersExceededWarning
problems.cause: Too many listeners registered on a single event, often due to listeners not being removed when modules are destroyed.
problems.solution: Call emitter.removeListener() or use the unsubscribe function returned by on(). Set emitter.setMaxListeners() to an appropriate value.

8.8.1.intuition: The CAP theorem is like a package delivery service during a snowstorm (network partition). You can either guarantee the package arrives with the correct contents (consistency) but it might not arrive at all, or guarantee the package arrives (availability) but it might contain yesterday's version of the contents. You cannot guarantee both during the storm. The best services offer different guarantees for different package types — express (strong consistency) for legal documents, standard (eventual consistency) for catalogs.

exercises.title: Consistency Model Simulator
exercises.description: Build a simulation with two Node.js processes representing database nodes. Simulate a network partition and demonstrate the difference between CP and AP behavior.
exercises.hint: Use two Express servers with shared state via HTTP. Simulate partition by blocking inter-node communication. CP: return error on reads. AP: return stale data.

exercises.title: Read-Your-Writes Implementation
exercises.description: Build a data access layer that provides read-your-writes consistency using a primary database and replica. Track per-user write timestamps in Redis.
exercises.hint: After a write, store userId:timestamp in Redis with 30s TTL. Before reads, check if user wrote recently — if so, read from primary.

exercises.title: Eventual Consistency Demo
exercises.description: Create a system where writes go to a primary and reads go to a replica. Measure replication lag and demonstrate how stale reads occur. Add a UI that shows the consistency gap.
exercises.hint: Write to primary, immediately read from replica and primary. Compare results. Show the lag decreasing over time.

problems.problem: Users see stale data after updating their profile
problems.cause: Writes go to primary database, but reads come from a replica with replication lag.
problems.solution: Implement read-your-writes consistency: after a write, route that user's subsequent reads to the primary for a few seconds.

problems.problem: Inventory oversold during flash sale
problems.cause: Eventual consistency in the inventory system allows two servers to both read 'in stock' before either write 'out of stock'.
problems.solution: Use strong consistency (database transaction with row locking) for inventory decrements. Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE or atomic decrement.

further_reading.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
further_reading.note: Chapters 5-9 cover consistency and replication in depth

further_reading.title: Jepsen - Distributed Systems Testing
further_reading.note: Testing framework that finds consistency bugs in distributed databases

8.8.2.intuition: Distributed consensus is like a group of friends trying to decide on a restaurant over a flaky group chat. Raft's approach: one person becomes the 'organizer' (leader) by getting a majority vote. The organizer proposes a restaurant, and everyone agrees if the organizer is still in charge. If the organizer goes offline, the group votes for a new organizer. The key rule: decisions only count if a majority participates.

exercises.title: Leader Election with etcd
exercises.description: Implement leader election using etcd. Run 3 Node.js instances that compete for leadership. When the leader stops, another instance should take over within the TTL.
exercises.hint: Use etcd lease with TTL. Put-if-absent for leader key. Watch for key deletion to trigger re-election.

exercises.title: Raft Visualization
exercises.description: Build a simple web-based Raft visualization with 5 nodes. Show leader election, heartbeats, and what happens when the leader crashes.
exercises.hint: Use Express + WebSocket. Each 'node' is a state machine. Visualize state transitions with different colors.

exercises.title: Split Brain Detector
exercises.description: Create a monitoring system that detects split-brain scenarios in a cluster. Alert when multiple nodes claim leadership simultaneously.
exercises.hint: Each leader writes its ID and timestamp to a shared store. Check for multiple recent leader claims.

problems.problem: Two nodes both acting as leader (split brain)
problems.cause: Network partition isolated the old leader from the majority. The majority elected a new leader, but the old leader doesn't know and continues operating.
problems.solution: Leaders must verify their lease/term before performing write operations. Use fencing tokens that increase with each leadership change. Old leaders with stale tokens are rejected.

problems.problem: Cluster cannot elect a leader
problems.cause: Even number of nodes causes tied votes. Or too many nodes are down, preventing a majority.
problems.solution: Always use an odd number of nodes (3, 5, 7). Ensure at least (N/2 + 1) nodes are healthy for the cluster to function.

further_reading.title: The Raft Paper
further_reading.note: Original Raft paper - surprisingly readable

further_reading.title: Raft Visualization
further_reading.note: Animated visualization of the Raft algorithm

further_reading.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
further_reading.note: Chapter 9 covers consensus in detail

8.8.3.intuition: Idempotency is like an elevator button. No matter how many times you press 'Floor 5', you only go to the 5th floor once. The elevator remembers the request and ignores duplicates. The Outbox pattern is like writing a letter and putting it in your outbox tray on your desk. A mail clerk periodically picks up letters and sends them. If the clerk is sick, the letters wait safely in the tray until the clerk returns — nothing is lost.

exercises.title: Idempotency Middleware
exercises.description: Build Express middleware that handles idempotency keys from the Idempotency-Key header. Cache results in Redis and return cached responses for duplicate requests.
exercises.hint: Extract key from header. Check Redis for existing result. If found, return it. If not, process request, store result, return it.

exercises.title: Outbox Pattern Implementation
exercises.description: Implement the Outbox pattern: write events to an outbox table in the same transaction as business data. Build a publisher that polls for unpublished events and sends them to Kafka.
exercises.hint: Use Prisma $transaction to write data + outbox event atomically. Separate process polls outbox table.

exercises.title: Idempotent API Design
exercises.description: Design and implement a payment API that is fully idempotent. Handle duplicate charges, network timeouts, and concurrent requests for the same payment.
exercises.hint: Use orderId as natural idempotency key. Unique constraint on order_id in payments table. Return existing payment for duplicates.

problems.problem: Duplicate records created from retried POST requests
problems.cause: POST endpoint is not idempotent. Network timeout causes client to retry, creating a second record.
problems.solution: Require Idempotency-Key header on POST requests. Check key before processing. Return cached result for duplicates.
problems.code: const check = await redis.get(`idempotent:${key}`);\nif (check) return res.json(JSON.parse(check));

problems.problem: Events published but business data write failed
problems.cause: Publishing an event to Kafka before the database transaction commits. If the transaction rolls back, the event is orphaned.
problems.solution: Use the Outbox pattern: write events to a database table in the same transaction. A separate publisher reads and sends them to Kafka.

further_reading.title: Idempotency Keys - Stripe
further_reading.note: How Stripe implements idempotent API requests

further_reading.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
further_reading.note: Chapter 11 covers reliable event processing

8.9.1.intuition: Distributed tracing is like a package tracking system. When you ship a package (request), it gets a tracking number (trace ID). At each facility (service) it passes through, a scan is recorded (span). You can follow the package's entire journey — see where it was, how long each stop took, and where it got stuck — all from one tracking number.

exercises.title: OpenTelemetry Setup
exercises.description: Set up OpenTelemetry with auto-instrumentation for an Express + PostgreSQL + Redis application. Configure Jaeger as the trace backend and visualize request traces.
exercises.hint: Import tracing.js before all other imports. Use getNodeAutoInstrumentations(). Start Jaeger with Docker.

exercises.title: Cross-Service Tracing
exercises.description: Build two microservices that communicate via HTTP. Set up trace context propagation so a single trace spans both services. View the connected trace in Jaeger.
exercises.hint: Auto-instrumentation handles HTTP propagation. Both services need OTel SDK configured with the same collector endpoint.

exercises.title: Custom Business Metrics
exercises.description: Add custom OpenTelemetry metrics to an order service: order count (counter), order value (histogram), and active orders (up-down counter). View in Prometheus/Grafana.
exercises.hint: Use meter.createCounter(), createHistogram(), createUpDownCounter(). Add labels for dimensions like order type and region.

problems.problem: Traces show disconnected spans (no parent-child relationship)
problems.cause: Trace context is not being propagated between services. Services create independent root spans.
problems.solution: Ensure HTTP auto-instrumentation is enabled (it propagates traceparent header). For message queues, manually inject/extract trace context.

problems.problem: Auto-instrumentation not capturing database queries
problems.cause: OpenTelemetry SDK was initialized after the database client was imported.
problems.solution: Import and initialize the tracing module as the very first import in your application entry point, before any other modules.

8.9.2.body:
# SLIs, SLOs, SLAs: Defining Reliability Targets

## Introduction

How reliable should your system be? 99.9%? 99.99%? The answer depends on user expectations, business impact, and engineering cost. SLIs (Service Level Indicators), SLOs (Service Level Objectives), and SLAs (Service Level Agreements) provide a framework for defining, measuring, and communicating reliability targets.

## The Reliability Pyramid

```
┌─────────┐
│   SLA   │  Business contract with customers
│(external│  "If we break this, we pay you"
│ promise)│
├─────────┤
│   SLO   │  Internal target
│(internal│  "We aim for this reliability level"
│ target) │  Stricter than SLA
├─────────┤
│   SLI   │  Actual measurement
│(measured │  "This is what we're actually achieving"
│  metric) │
└─────────┘
```

## SLIs (Service Level Indicators)

SLIs are quantitative measures of service behavior:

```
Common SLIs:
┌─────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐
│ SLI Type        │ Measurement                          │
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Availability    │ % of requests that succeed            │
│ Latency         │ % of requests faster than threshold   │
│ Throughput      │ Requests processed per second          │
│ Error rate      │ % of requests that return errors       │
│ Correctness     │ % of responses with correct data       │
│ Freshness       │ % of data updated within threshold     │
└─────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Measuring SLIs in Node.js

```javascript
import { metrics } from '@opentelemetry/api';
import client from 'prom-client';

// Prometheus metrics
const httpRequestDuration = new client.Histogram({
  name: 'http_request_duration_seconds',
  help: 'HTTP request duration in seconds',
  labelNames: ['method', 'route', 'status_code'],
  buckets: [0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10],
});

const httpRequestsTotal = new client.Counter({
  name: 'http_requests_total',
  help: 'Total HTTP requests',
  labelNames: ['method', 'route', 'status_code'],
});

const httpErrorsTotal = new client.Counter({
  name: 'http_errors_total',
  help: 'Total HTTP errors (5xx)',
  labelNames: ['method', 'route'],
});

// Middleware to collect SLIs
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  const start = process.hrtime.bigint();

  res.on('finish', () => {
    const durationMs = Number(process.hrtime.bigint() - start) / 1e6;
    const durationS = durationMs / 1000;
    const route = req.route?.path || req.path;

    httpRequestDuration.observe(
      { method: req.method, route, status_code: res.statusCode },
      durationS
    );

    httpRequestsTotal.inc(
      { method: req.method, route, status_code: res.statusCode }
    );

    if (res.statusCode >= 500) {
      httpErrorsTotal.inc({ method: req.method, route });
    }
  });

  next();
});

// Expose metrics endpoint
app.get('/metrics', async (req, res) => {
  res.set('Content-Type', client.register.contentType);
  res.end(await client.register.metrics());
});
```

## SLOs (Service Level Objectives)

SLOs are targets for your SLIs:

```
Example SLOs for an API service:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ SLI               │ SLO                             │
├───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Availability      │ 99.9% of requests return 2xx/4xx│
│ Latency (p50)     │ 50% of requests < 100ms         │
│ Latency (p99)     │ 99% of requests < 500ms         │
│ Error rate        │ < 0.1% of requests return 5xx   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

99.9% availability = "three nines"
  = 8.76 hours of downtime per year
  = 43.8 minutes per month
  = 1.44 minutes per day

99.99% = "four nines"
  = 52.6 minutes per year
  = 4.38 minutes per month
```

### Error Budget

```
Error Budget = 1 - SLO target

If SLO = 99.9% availability:
  Error Budget = 0.1% = 43.8 minutes/month

Month starts: 43.8 minutes of allowed downtime
Week 1: 5-minute outage → 38.8 minutes remaining
Week 2: 2-minute outage → 36.8 minutes remaining
Week 3: No outages → 36.8 minutes remaining

Error budget remaining: 36.8 minutes
  → Safe to deploy risky changes

If error budget is exhausted:
  → Freeze feature deployments
  → Focus on reliability improvements
```

```javascript
// Error budget calculator
class ErrorBudgetTracker {
  constructor(sloTarget, windowDays = 30) {
    this.sloTarget = sloTarget;
    this.windowMs = windowDays * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000;
  }

  async calculate(prometheusClient) {
    const windowStart = Date.now() - this.windowMs;

    // Query actual availability from Prometheus
    const totalRequests = await prometheusClient.query(
      `sum(increase(http_requests_total[${this.windowMs / 1000}s]))`
    );
    const errorRequests = await prometheusClient.query(
      `sum(increase(http_errors_total[${this.windowMs / 1000}s]))`
    );

    const actualAvailability = 1 - (errorRequests / totalRequests);
    const errorBudgetTotal = 1 - this.sloTarget;
    const errorBudgetUsed = 1 - actualAvailability;
    const errorBudgetRemaining = errorBudgetTotal - errorBudgetUsed;

    return {
      sloTarget: this.sloTarget,
      actualAvailability: (actualAvailability * 100).toFixed(3) + '%',
      errorBudgetTotal: (errorBudgetTotal * 100).toFixed(3) + '%',
      errorBudgetUsed: (errorBudgetUsed * 100).toFixed(3) + '%',
      errorBudgetRemaining: (errorBudgetRemaining * 100).toFixed(3) + '%',
      errorBudgetRemainingMinutes: (errorBudgetRemaining * this.windowMs / 60000).toFixed(1),
      canDeploy: errorBudgetRemaining > 0,
    };
  }
}
```

## SLAs (Service Level Agreements)

SLAs are contractual commitments with financial consequences:

```
Example SLA (simplified):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Service Availability │ Service Credit                    │
├──────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
│ >= 99.9%             │ None (within target)              │
│ 99.0% - 99.9%       │ 10% credit on monthly bill        │
│ 95.0% - 99.0%       │ 25% credit on monthly bill        │
│ < 95.0%              │ 50% credit on monthly bill        │
└──────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘

SLO should always be stricter than SLA:
  SLA: 99.9% availability (contractual)
  SLO: 99.95% availability (internal target)
  Gap: 0.05% = buffer before SLA breach
```

## Choosing the Right SLOs

```
Questions to ask:
1. What do users actually care about?
   → Availability and latency, usually

2. What latency is "good enough"?
   → Measure current performance, set targets slightly above

3. What's the cost of each nine?
   → Each additional nine roughly doubles engineering cost

Service Type and Suggested SLOs:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Service Type          │ Availability │ Latency (p99)   │
├───────────────────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┤
│ Payment processing    │ 99.99%       │ < 1s            │
│ User-facing API       │ 99.9%        │ < 500ms         │
│ Internal API          │ 99.5%        │ < 1s            │
│ Batch processing      │ 99%          │ N/A             │
│ Analytics dashboard   │ 99%          │ < 5s            │
│ Email sending         │ 99.5%        │ < 30s           │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Grafana Dashboard for SLOs

```
Prometheus queries for SLO dashboard:

Availability (last 30 days):
  1 - (
    sum(rate(http_errors_total[30d]))
    /
    sum(rate(http_requests_total[30d]))
  )

Latency P99 (last 5 minutes):
  histogram_quantile(0.99,
    sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)
  )

Error budget remaining:
  (1 - 0.999) - (
    sum(rate(http_errors_total[30d]))
    /
    sum(rate(http_requests_total[30d]))
  )

Burn rate (how fast error budget is being consumed):
  sum(rate(http_errors_total[1h])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total[1h]))
  /
  (1 - 0.999)
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Too many SLOs**: Start with 2-3 SLOs per service. More creates confusion and alert fatigue.
2. **SLOs that don't reflect user experience**: "CPU usage < 80%" is not an SLO. "99% of requests < 500ms" is.
3. **No error budget policy**: Without consequences for exhausting the error budget, SLOs become meaningless.
4. **Setting SLOs without data**: Measure first, then set targets based on actual performance and user expectations.

## Summary

SLIs measure your service's behavior (latency, availability, error rate). SLOs set targets for those measurements ("99.9% availability"). SLAs are contractual commitments with financial consequences. Error budgets translate SLOs into actionable deployment decisions. Start by measuring SLIs, set SLOs based on user expectations, and use error budgets to balance reliability with feature velocity.
8.9.2.intuition: SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs are like a driving analogy. SLIs are your speedometer reading (actual measurement). SLOs are the speed limit you set for yourself, say 65 mph (internal target). SLAs are the posted legal speed limit of 70 mph (contractual, with penalties). You aim for 65 so you have buffer before getting a ticket at 70. The error budget is how many mph you can go over your personal limit before hitting the legal limit.

exercises.title: SLI Collection Middleware
exercises.description: Build Express middleware that collects availability and latency SLIs using Prometheus client. Expose a /metrics endpoint and visualize in Grafana.
exercises.hint: Use prom-client Histogram for latency and Counter for total/error requests. Label by route and status code.

exercises.title: Error Budget Calculator
exercises.description: Build a dashboard that shows current error budget for a 99.9% availability SLO over a 30-day window. Show budget remaining in minutes and percentage.
exercises.hint: Query total and error request counts for the window. Calculate: budget = 0.1% - actual_error_rate.

exercises.title: SLO-Based Deployment Gate
exercises.description: Create a CI/CD check that blocks deployments when the error budget is less than 25% remaining. Query Prometheus for current SLO compliance.
exercises.hint: Build a script that queries Prometheus API. If error_budget_remaining < 0.25 * error_budget_total, exit with error code.

problems.problem: Alert fatigue from too many SLO-based alerts
problems.cause: Alerting on every SLO violation creates noise. Minor, transient blips trigger alerts.
problems.solution: Use burn rate alerting: alert when the error budget is being consumed faster than expected (e.g., would exhaust in 6 hours at current rate), not on individual violations.

problems.problem: SLO shows 99.95% but users are complaining
problems.cause: SLI doesn't capture what users actually experience. Average latency is good but p99 is terrible, or errors only affect a specific user segment.
problems.solution: Measure SLIs at the user-journey level, not just the endpoint level. Include p99 latency, not just p50. Segment by user type or geography.

further_reading.title: SLO Burn Rate Alerting
further_reading.note: Google's approach to SLO-based alerting

8.9.3.intuition: Alerting is like a home security system. Bad alerting is a system that beeps every time a leaf blows past the window (alert fatigue). Good alerting only sounds when someone is actually trying to break in (user impact). The burn rate approach is like tracking how many suspicious events happened in the last hour — one leaf is fine, but a pattern of increasing activity triggers an alert.

exercises.title: Burn Rate Alert Configuration
exercises.description: Configure Prometheus alert rules for a 99.9% availability SLO using multi-window burn rate alerting. Set up fast burn (pages) and slow burn (tickets) alerts.
exercises.hint: Fast burn: 14.4x rate over 1h window. Slow burn: 3x rate over 6h window. Use the error_rate / (1 - SLO) formula.

exercises.title: Health Check System
exercises.description: Build a comprehensive health check system that checks database, Redis, and external API dependencies. Expose separate liveness and readiness endpoints for Kubernetes.
exercises.hint: Liveness: always 200 if process is running. Readiness: 200 only if all critical dependencies are healthy.

exercises.title: Automated Runbook
exercises.description: Create an automated runbook system that executes diagnostic and remediation steps when specific alerts fire. Log all actions taken for post-mortem review.
exercises.hint: Map alert names to step arrays. Each step has a check and an action. Stop at first failed step and alert human.

problems.problem: On-call person paged 10+ times per week
problems.cause: Alerts are too sensitive (low thresholds), too many non-actionable alerts, or flapping alerts that trigger repeatedly.
problems.solution: Review and tune alert thresholds. Remove alerts that are never acted upon. Add appropriate 'for' duration (e.g., 5m) to prevent flapping. Use burn rate alerting instead of threshold alerts.

problems.problem: Incidents keep recurring without improvement
problems.cause: No post-mortem process, or post-mortems happen but action items are not tracked.
problems.solution: Mandate blameless post-mortems for all P1/P2 incidents. Track action items in a project management tool. Review completion in weekly team meetings.

further_reading.title: Alerting on SLOs - Google SRE Workbook
further_reading.note: Multi-window burn rate alerting explained

further_reading.title: PagerDuty Incident Response
further_reading.note: Open-source incident response guide
